attributes.stem:
A 28-year-old woman presents to the antenatal clinic for review of her morphology ultrasound performed at 20 weeks gestation. This is her first pregnancy. She has no significant medical history and takes only pregnancy vitamins. She has no symptoms and feels well with normal fetal movements. The morphology ultrasound shows normal fetal anatomy. Transvaginal ultrasound shows a cervical length of 20 mm with no funnelling.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Her abdomen is soft and non-tender with a gravid uterus palpable at the umbilicus.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management?
options: Expectant management with repeat cervical length in 2 weeks
attributes.explanation:
This patient has an incidentally detected short cervix on morphology ultrasound. A cervical length less than 25 mm at 20-24 weeks gestation is associated with an increased risk of spontaneous preterm birth. The shorter the cervical length, the higher the risk of preterm birth.

Vaginal progesterone is the most appropriate management for asymptomatic women with a cervical length less than 25 mm detected on ultrasound between 16 and 24 weeks gestation. Multiple randomised controlled trials have shown that vaginal progesterone reduces the risk of preterm birth and improves neonatal outcomes in this population.

Cervical cerclage would be considered if there was a history of previous preterm births or mid-trimester losses, but is not first-line therapy for an incidentally detected short cervix in a first pregnancy without symptoms.

Bed rest and pelvic rest have not been shown to reduce the risk of preterm birth in women with a short cervix and may increase the risk of venous thromboembolism and deconditioning.

Expectant management with routine follow-up alone would be inadequate given the proven benefit of progesterone therapy in reducing preterm birth risk in this situation.

Expectant management with repeat cervical length in 2 weeks without intervention would delay the initiation of proven preventive therapy and potentially increase the risk of preterm birth.

In addition to starting vaginal progesterone, these patients should have regular monitoring of cervical length and be educated about the signs and symptoms of preterm labour. They should be referred to a specialist obstetric service for ongoing care.
learningPoints: A short cervix (<25mm) on transvaginal ultrasound at 20-24 weeks gestation is a significant risk factor for preterm birth.
Vaginal progesterone is the first-line preventive therapy for asymptomatic women with a short cervix.
Regular monitoring of cervical length is indicated in women with a short cervix detected on morphology ultrasound.
references: Royal Australian and New Zealand College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists (RANZCOG). Measurement of cervical length for prediction of preterm birth [Internet]. Melbourne: RANZCOG; 2023 [cited 2024 June 7]. Available from: https://ranzcog.edu.au/wp-content/uploads/Measurement-Cervical-Length-Preterm-Birth.pdf
National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE). Preterm labour and birth [Internet]. London: NICE; 2015 [cited 2024 June 7]. Available from: https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ng25

Queensland Health. Guideline for premature rupture of membranes at term [Internet]. Brisbane: Queensland Health; 2021 [cited 2024 June 7]. Available from: https://www.health.qld.gov.au/__data/assets/pdf_file/0019/140149/g-ptl.pdf
